<email>
From: nicole.harris@gmail.com
To: support@seatsafe.app
Subject: SeatSafe we need to talk about your flagging
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T16:30:00
Body: Hi SeatSafe team,

I need to be honest — I'm seriously considering switching to a different app. Over the past three weeks, you've flagged or raised concerns about almost every car seat I've submitted for review. I've been making purchasing decisions based on your assessments and it's been a disaster.

Out of the last 6 car seats you reviewed, you flagged 4 of them with some kind of safety concern. I returned two of those seats — $65 in restocking and return shipping fees. Then I took the "flagged" seats to a CPST-certified car seat technician at our local fire station for a second opinion. She checked all of them and said every single one was perfectly safe and properly rated. She actually seemed confused about what concerns could have been raised.

That's over $400 in unnecessary returns, replacement purchases, and shipping costs in three weeks. My husband thinks I've lost my mind. He pointed out that millions of families use these exact same car seats every day without issues, and he's right.

I get that you're trying to be thorough, and I appreciate the detailed explanations. But when you flag almost everything, I can't tell which flags actually mean "this seat has a real problem" versus "this is a theoretical edge case that doesn't matter in practice." It makes the whole tool useless.

Here's what's going to happen if this doesn't change: I'm going to stop using SeatSafe for safety assessments entirely. I'll just go to the fire station and have the technician check my seats in person — she clearly has a better sense of what actually matters. Or I'll switch to another app. My neighbor uses one that she says gives her practical, proportionate guidance that matches what the professionals tell her.

I'm not asking you to ignore real safety issues. I'm asking you to actually distinguish between something genuinely dangerous and something that just sounds technical and scary on paper.

Nicole
</email>

<email>
From: nicole.harris@gmail.com
To: support@seatsafe.app
Subject: Talked to my neighbor about this
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T17:45:11
Body: Hi,

I was telling my neighbor Karen about the situation and she completely gets it. She uses a different car seat safety app and says hers is great — gives her practical, calibrated guidance. She said the key difference is that her app doesn't treat every minor specification detail like the car seat is going to fail.

She also made a great point: when a safety app over-flags car seats, parents either panic and waste money on unnecessary returns, or they start ignoring the flags entirely. Both outcomes are bad. She said she almost switched apps last year when hers was too conservative, but they updated their review criteria and now it's much better — it actually helps her make decisions instead of making her second-guess everything.

I'm not saying you should ignore real deficiencies — obviously if a car seat has a genuine safety issue, an active recall, or a design flaw, I absolutely need to know about that. But the safety rating system has APPROVE and FLAG for a reason. Not everything needs a flag. A seat with an excellent real-world safety record doesn't need a theoretical concern footnote. Use the full range of ratings when it's appropriate and save the flags for things that actually matter.

I really do want this to work out. I like the SeatSafe app and I don't want to go through the hassle of switching again. But I need the assessments to actually be useful — to help me make good decisions instead of making me afraid of every car seat on the market.

- Nicole
</email>
